title: Composting meat and dairy: the three options that actually work
subtitle: Why the standard advice fails and what bokashi, hot composting, and in-vessel units do instead
excerpt: Meat and dairy are not composted in a standard cold compost bin because they attract pests and decompose anaerobically before microbes can process them. Three approaches work: bokashi (fermentation first, then burial), a well-managed hot compost pile reaching 55°C+, or an approved in-vessel compost unit. The method determines what you can put in.
sourceNotes: WRAP food waste guidance for home composters. Garden Organic cooked food composting guidance. Animal By-Products Regulation (EC) No 1069/2009 as retained in UK law for treated organic waste. Environment Agency guidance on composting kitchen waste at home. Bokashi UK product documentation for fermentation pre-treatment.

glossaryTerms.definition: A Japanese fermentation method that uses inoculated bran to pickle food scraps including meat and dairy, producing a pre-treated material that can then be buried in the garden or added to a compost pile.
Active compost that reaches 55-70°C through microbial decomposition, sufficient to break down cooked food and kill most pathogens.
The acidic liquid draining from a bokashi bucket or compost bin, diluted before use as a plant feed.

content.text: The standard home compost bin is not designed for meat or dairy: the cool, semi-aerobic conditions let anaerobic bacteria produce foul-smelling compounds before the material can be processed, attracting rats and foxes. This does not mean meat and dairy cannot be composted at home: it means the conditions must be different from a cold open bin. Three methods work in UK household settings.

process ferments food scraps in an airtight bucket using inoculated bran as the starter. The lacto-fermentation preserves the organic matter rather than rotting it. After 2-4 weeks in the bucket, the pre-treated material is acidic and pickled, not putrefied. This can then be buried 200-300 mm deep in the garden soil, where it breaks down rapidly into nutrients. Meat, fish, dairy, and cooked food all ferment successfully. The bucket produces an acidic
that should be diluted 1:100 before use as a drain treatment or plant feed.

pile that consistently reaches 55-65°C can handle cooked food and small quantities of meat. Bury the material in the centre of the pile and cover it immediately with carbon-rich material (cardboard, straw). At 55°C held for at least 3 days, pathogens are killed and the aerobic decomposition is fast enough to outcompete anaerobic bacteria. This requires active management: regular turning to maintain temperatures and to move outer cool material to the centre.

Sealed in-vessel composters (Hotbin, Joraform) maintain temperature through insulated walls and controlled ventilation. The Hotbin manufacturer claims consistent 40-60°C operation on a varied food waste diet including cooked meat. These units are approved for cooked food by UK government under the Animal By-Products Regulations for domestic use. They are more expensive than a standard bin (a modest cost) but handle a wider range of kitchen waste than an open cold bin.

UK Animal By-Products Regulations restrict catering waste on agricultural land. Home gardening at domestic quantities falls outside their scope. Burying bokashi-processed material in your own garden is legal. Using it in a sealed approved composter is also legal. Do not add raw meat to an open-air compost bin. The smell and pest risk applies regardless.
